architecture.summary: The system performs constrained decoding of diffusion language models by checking at each generation step whether a proposed token insertion maintains validity with respect to a context-free grammar (CFG). The mechanism works by reducing the constrained decoding problem to deciding whether the intersection of the target CFG and a regular language (representing all possible completions of the partial output) is non-empty. At each step, the diffusion model proposes inserting a token at a specific location in the partial output; the system constructs a DFA encoding all valid completions, intersects it with the CFG, and either accepts the proposal (if intersection is non-empty) or rejects and resamples. The system optionally completes aborted generations by sampling uniformly from the intersection language.

biblio.title: Constrained Decoding of Diffusion LLMs with Context-Free Grammars

claims.statement: For multi-region infilling on 1-mri, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness by an absolute increase ranging between 6.2 and 9.5 percentage points per model
For 2-mri, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness from 51.5–66.3% (vanilla) to 72.9–86.5% (constrained), with completion reaching 93.1–99.0%

claims.dataset: C++ code generation from natural language (HumanEval-based)
claims.statement: For diffusion language models on C++, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness from 11.0–40.5% (vanilla) to 19.0–58.7% (constrained -), and to 99.2–99.7% with completion
For JSON structured data extraction with diffusion models, constrained decoding with completion achieves 100% syntactic correctness across all tested models
For 1-mri infilling, functional correctness (pass@1) increases on average from vanilla to constrained decoding by 2.3 percentage points, and with completion to 2.8 percentage points

claims.dataset: C++ infilling across model sizes (1.3B to 33B parameters)
claims.statement: Median runtime overhead of constrained decoding is 51%, with overhead on 1.3B models higher (75%) than on 7B models (50%) and 33B models (48%)

claims.dataset: C++, JSON, SMILES generation with diffusion models
claims.statement: For diffusion language models, median completion overhead is only 6%, with speed-ups observed up to 60% (when decoding is preemptively aborted) and slowdowns up to 80%

Remaining syntax errors when using constrained decoding without completion (Con.-) are attributed to overapproximation: the regular language construction allows arbitrary token fills, but in practice the model is limited by maximum token budget (fim/mri) or mask tokens (dlm); accurately modeling remaining tokens increases intersection language size prohibitively, rendering the method too expensive
